title: Chapter 37: Code vs Case Law
content: Three days.

Three days of checking my phone every fourteen minutes like a disaster girl refreshing earthquake alerts. Three days of Evelyn telling me to stay calm, stay ready, stay off social media—which, rude, but also fair, because I'd definitely have posted something incriminating by now. Three days of feeling everything, all at once, like someone cracked open a fire hydrant inside my chest and forgot where the valve was.

I'm sitting in Evelyn's office when the call comes. The light through the window is that particular late-afternoon gold that makes Manhattan look almost soft, almost kind, almost like a city that wouldn't hollow out a twenty-three-year-old and sell the contents. I'm fidgeting with a pen—the click-click-click of it probably driving Evelyn insane, but she hasn't said anything, because even Evelyn Hart has limits to her assertiveness and apparently my nervous energy is one of them.
Her desk phone rings.
Landline. Who still has a landline? Lawyers. Lawyers still have landlines.

Evelyn picks up on the second ring. Her face doesn't change—years of depositions have given her the emotional expressiveness of a granite countertop—but I watch her fingers tighten on the receiver. Just slightly. Just enough.
«When?» A pause. «Understood. We'll be there.»
She hangs up. Doesn't move. Doesn't blink. She goes so still that for a half-second I wonder if the call was bad news, if everything crashed and burned, if Mephisto won somehow—

«That was Judge Morrison's clerk.» Evelyn's voice is precise. Measured. But there's something underneath it, something I can feel now because my emotional range isn't a throttled modem anymore. It's anticipation. «The ruling will be issued at four PM today.»
My heart slams into my ribs.

I can feel it—I can *feel* it—the adrenaline flooding my system like warm water, the fear and the hope and the sick excitement all braiding together in my gut. Three weeks ago I couldn't have told you what hope felt like. Now it's sitting on my sternum like a cat that outweighs me.
«Be in the courtroom at three forty-five,» Evelyn says. She's already standing, already reaching for her coat. «Wear the navy suit.»
The navy suit. The one she bought me. The one that fits like armor.
I stand up, and my legs are shaking, and I don't care, because at least I can feel them shaking. At least I can feel *something.*

The courtroom smells like old wood and fresh ink—varnish that's been polished since before my grandmother was born and the chemical tang of a printer that just spat out forty-seven pages of ruling. I breathe it in: cedar and solvent and the faint mustiness of legal precedent accumulated like dust. It grounds me. Strange, that a room that's terrified me for months now feels like the only safe place in the city.

Same wood paneling. Same flag in the corner, its colors muted under fluorescent light. Same counsel tables where Lessler sat last week, peddling his jurisdiction argument like a used car salesman with a law degree. He's not here today. No one's here for the defense. The gallery is half-empty—reporters, a few law students, two women I don't recognize who have the look of class members, people whose emotions were extracted right alongside mine.

Evelyn sits beside me. Straight-spined. Immaculate. Her hand rests on the table, and I notice—only because I'm looking for it—the faintest tremor in her fingers. She's nervous too. She'd never admit it. But she is.
The door opens.

Judge Morrison enters. She's carrying a document—the ruling. It's thick. I can see the weight of it in how she holds it, one hand under the spine like it matters, like the words inside are heavy enough to matter. Forty-seven pages. She sets it on the bench and the sound it makes is a soft, authoritative thud.
«Please be seated.» Her voice is measured. Judicial. Careful in the way that judges are careful when they know they're about to drop something that'll get cited for the next thirty years.
She begins to read.

«This matter comes before the Court on Plaintiff's motion for summary judgment and Defendant's motion to dismiss. The Court has considered the parties' submissions, the evidentiary record, and the applicable law, and renders the following decision.»
My hands are shaking. I can feel them shaking against the table—the cool lacquered wood under my palms, the vibration of my own pulse. I grip the edge of the counsel table and hold on.
Judge Morrison turns a page. The sound is crisp. Authoritative. Like a bone setting itself.

«The Court first addresses the Defendant's jurisdictional argument. Defendant contends that the contractual selection of the 'Infernal Court' as the forum for dispute resolution deprives this Court of jurisdiction.» She pauses. Looks up from the page. «The so-called Infernal Court is not a recognized judicial forum. A contract cannot create jurisdiction where none exists. The Defendant's forum selection clause is void as against public policy. Motion to dismiss on jurisdictional grounds: DENIED.»
The air in the room shifts. I can feel it—a collective exhalation, the reporters' pens moving faster. Evelyn's hand tightens on her legal pad.
Page by page. Judge Morrison dismantles Mephisto's defense like she's taking apart a clock, piece by piece, laying the gears on the table so everyone can see how badly they were assembled.

«The Court next considers the Defendant's reliance on the 1923 Uniform Commercial Code framework as the contract's governing law.» She reads the finding without inflection, but the words land like hammer blows. «The contract's governing law has been superseded by subsequent federal legislation, including but not limited to the AI Data Privacy Protection Act and the Consumer Protection from Digital Exploitation Act. The Defendant's reliance on superseded statutory authority is without merit. The 1923 UCC framework as applied to this contract: INVALIDATED.»
Invalidated. The word hangs in the air, bright and sharp. The 1923 framework—the entire legal architecture Mephisto built his enterprise on—is just... gone. A dead law for a dead deal.

I taste copper on my tongue. Adrenaline. My own body reacting to the ruling like it's a physical thing, like the words have weight and mass and they just hit me square in the chest. I swallow hard and keep listening.

«The Court now turns to the central question of the Defendant's extraction of emotional data.» Judge Morrison's voice takes on a different quality—not softer, but more deliberate, like she wants every syllable to land in the record with absolute precision. «The Defendant's extraction of emotional data constitutes non-consensual neural data harvesting in violation of 47 U.S.C. § 2305, the AI Data Privacy Protection Act, and 15 U.S.C. § 45(a), the Consumer Protection from Digital Exploitation Act. The extraction was conducted without informed consent, without adequate disclosure, and without the Plaintiffs' meaningful opportunity to opt out. The extraction: UNLAWFUL.»
Unlawful.
Not unethical. Not questionable. *Unlawful.*

I didn't realize I was crying until Evelyn pressed a tissue into my hand. She doesn't look at me. She doesn't need to. I dab my eyes and keep my hands on the table because I need to hear the rest. I need to hear every single word.
Judge Morrison turns another page.

«Having found the Defendant's conduct to be in violation of federal law, the Court further finds that the Defendant has been unjustly enriched through the systematic extraction and monetization of Plaintiff's and the Class's emotional data. The Defendant derived substantial revenue—»
She reads the number. I won't repeat it here because it's obscene. Nine figures. Money made from mining grief and joy and fear from people who didn't even know they'd been cracked open.

«Under Delaware General Corporation Law § 225, the Court orders the disgorgement of Defendant's controlling ownership interest in Mephisto MCN, LLC. Plaintiff is awarded 51% of the outstanding shares, effective immediately.»
Fifty-one percent.
I own the company.

The words don't compute at first—they float in front of me like a lagging video buffer, half-rendered, nonsensical. And then they snap into focus and I can feel it, I can feel *everything*: the shock like ice water, the disbelief like altitude, the wild, screaming, irrational joy that wants to climb out of my throat and announce itself to the entire Southern District of New York.
I own him. Not his soul—his *company.* The vehicle he used to harvest us. I'm driving it now.
Every screen in the courtroom flickers.

I notice it like you notice weather—the monitors on the walls, the display on the court reporter's station, the digital clock above the bench. A micro-hesitation. A stutter in the current. Nothing dramatic. Just enough to make the hair on my arms stand up.
Then my phone buzzes.
Not a notification. Not a text. A message sliding onto my lock screen from an app I never installed, in a font I've never seen:
[APPEAL FILED. STAY REQUESTED. ACCELERATED DEDUCTION PROTOCOL: REINITIALIZED.]

Every phone in the courtroom buzzes. Same message. Same black text on a red background. The reporters' laptops chime in unison—a sound like a hospital ward full of heart monitors. The security cameras in the corners of the room swivel, all four of them, angling toward the counsel table. Toward me.
Judge Morrison's own computer displays the message fullscreen. Her face goes rigid.
He didn't send counsel. He *is* the counsel. He's in the wires. He's in the walls. Mephisto is the building's nervous system and he just fired every synapse he has.
He's reactivating the extraction.

The unDRM—the modification Leo installed at the soul level of the network—is still in place. But Mephisto's trying to override it remotely, pushing a countercommand through the infrastructure he built, the server architecture he designed, the backdoors he probably installed before the ink was dry on my contract. And if the override reaches the boot sequence before the ruling is enforced—
My phone lights up. A real text this time. Leo.
*«He's counterattacking the unDRM. I can hold it but I need time.»*
My fingers are shaking. I type back: *«How much time?»*
Three dots. Then:
*«Ninety seconds. I need ninety seconds to lock the modification.»*
Ninety seconds.

A minute and a half. The same minute and a half that started my recovery—the unDRM's activation window, the ninety-second pulse that reconnected me to my own emotional spectrum. Now it's the window for protecting it. The universe has a sick sense of rhythm.
I look at Evelyn. She's already seen the screens. Her jaw is set like concrete. She gives me the smallest nod—the kind of nod that means *you know what to do.*
I do.
I stand up.

Every eye in the courtroom is on me. The cameras are pointed at me. I can feel Mephisto watching through them—his attention like a searchlight, cold and vast and furious. My heart is hammering so hard I can taste the pulse in my throat, copper and adrenaline and something else, something new: the absolute diamond-hard certainty that I am not the person I was when I signed that contract.

«Your Honor.» My voice doesn't shake. It should. It doesn't. «The Defendant is attempting to destroy evidence and reinstate unlawful extraction in real time. I request an immediate enforcement order.»

Judge Morrison looks at me. She looks at her computer screen—still blazing red with Mephisto's declaration. She looks at the cameras, which are still pointed at me, watching, recording, the enemy's eyes inside the temple of justice.
She picks up her gavel.

The sound it makes when it strikes the block is the loudest thing I've ever heard—not just loud, but *dense*, a crack that fills the room and echoes off the wood paneling and vibrates through the table and into my hands and up my arms and into whatever part of me is still capable of believing that the world can change.

«So ordered.» Her voice is iron. «U.S. Marshals will enforce the Court's order effective immediately. The Defendant's attempted interference with the Court's ruling is noted in the record and will be referred for criminal contempt proceedings.»
The gavel's echo is still bouncing around the room—wood and authority and the weight of the state behind it—when my phone lights up.
Leo's message: *«DONE. unDRM locked. Permanent modification confirmed.»*

I read it three times. Permanent. The word I've been waiting for since a blood-red notification told me my joy was being processed. Permanent. The extraction is dead. Not paused, not stayed, not pending appeal—*dead*, locked at the root level, and no amount of remote override is going to bring it back.
The ruling is law. I own fifty-one percent. The unDRM is permanent.
We won.

I sit back down. My hands are still shaking—grief and relief and rage and exhaustion all duking it out in my knuckles. Evelyn puts her hand over mine. Just for a second. Just long enough for me to feel that her fingers are trembling too. Then she pulls back, straightens her jacket, and becomes granite again.
But I felt it. The tremor. The pride she'll never voice.

Judge Morrison is wrapping up, ordering the clerk to file the ruling, scheduling a conference for damages calculation, establishing the framework for the class-wide remedy. I hear the words but they're floating now, beautiful and surreal, and under all of it is a current I can't ignore.
Mephisto is still in the building.

He's in the wires. The servers. The screens. The cameras that tracked me are still tracking me—he's watching through them right now, probably, cataloguing, planning, adjusting. He filed an appeal in the time it takes a human to blink. He'll file another. And another. He has resources I can't imagine and patience measured in centuries.
The ruling is law. But the appeal is a clock.

Ninety days to file. Ninety days to find a circuit court that'll listen. Ninety days to dig up every loophole, every precedent, every technicality in a legal system he's been gaming since before the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure existed. And he'll use every single one.
Ninety seconds made me whole. The ninety days ahead could unmake me.

I look at the flag in the corner. I look at the wood paneling, old and solid and indifferent. I look at my mother, who just won the case of her career, and at my phone, where Leo's *«DONE»* glows like a lighthouse.
We won. The extraction is dead. I own the company.
But Mephisto is still in the walls. And ninety days is an eternity when your opponent has been playing this game since before computers had screens.

The gavel's echo has finally faded. The courtroom is emptying. Reporters are already filing their stories—*Historic Ruling in AI Emotion Harvesting Case*—and I'm still sitting here, still shaking, still tasting copper and adrenaline and something that might be the first clean fear I've felt in years.
Fear that knows what it's afraid of.
Fear that's ready.
